narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 62% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction considers key aspects of your background, like your bottom-decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 school grades—far below the typical student's mid-range position—along with your low LSAT score of 27.5 compared to the average of 36.7, low family income bracket, Native American racial background (which is underrepresented at just 1.8% in the data), solid undergraduate GPA of 3.5 (above the 3.21 average), male gender, and full-time study plans. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which had the biggest push toward predicting failure since it signals weaker academic performance relative to peers. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also pushing in the same unfavorable direction due to its low standing. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, further tilting the prediction toward failure because it's well below what's typical for bar passers. Family income came next, with your low bracket contributing negatively, followed by your racial background as the fifth-most influential factor, both nudging the odds against passing. Even your stronger undergraduate GPA played a role as sixth in impact, though it pushed toward failure in this context, while your full-time status and male gender had the least sway. Overall, these top factors—especially early and later school deciles and LSAT—painted a picture riskier than most applicants, who tend to have higher deciles, LSATs around 37, and mid-level family incomes. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a changeable factor with proven impact. You could also build a stronger academic record through additional coursework or certifications showing improvement beyond your past deciles. While family income and background are harder to shift, demonstrating dedication via test prep or bar-focused study habits could help offset those in a reapplication. Keep pushing forward—you have strengths like your UGPA that stand out, and targeted improvements can rewrite this story.
